coredata.dc:title: Effect of the fluorination of DLC film on the corrosion protection of aluminum alloy (AA 5052)
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2010 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Rangel, R.C.C.
Souza, M.E.P.
Schreiner, W.H.
Freire, C.M.A.
Rangel, E.C.
Cruz, N.C.
coredata.dc:description: It is presented a study conducted on the physical and electrochemical properties of fluorinated a-C:H films deposited onto a commercial aluminum alloy (AA 5052). The coatings were deposited from mixtures of 91% of acetylene and 9% of argon by plasma immersion ion implantation and deposition technique, PIIID. Total gas pressure was 44Pa and deposition time (t dep) was varied from 300 to 1200s. The depositing plasmas were generated by the application of radiofrequency power (13.56MHz, 100W) to the upper electrode and high voltage negative pulses (2400V, 300Hz) to the sample holder. Fluorine was incorporated in a post-deposition plasma treatment (13.56MHz, 70W, 13Pa) generated from sulfur hexafluoride atmosphere. Chemical structure and composition of the films were investigated using infrared reflectance/absorbance spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The corrosion resistance of the layers was determined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a 3.5% NaCl solution, at room temperature. Films presented good adhesion to the substrates and are classified as hydrogenated amorphous carbon (a-C:H) with oxygen traces. Fluorine was detected in all the samples after the post-deposition treatment being its proportion independent on the deposition time. Film thickness presented different tendencies with t dep revealing the variation of the deposition rate as a function of the deposition time. Such fluorinated a-C:H films improved the corrosion resistance of the aluminum surface. In a general way the corrosion resistance was higher for films prepared with lower deposition times. The variation of sample temperature with t dep was found to be decisive for the concentration of defects in the films and, consequently, for the performance of the samples in electrochemical tests. Results are interpreted in terms of the energy delivered to the growing layer by ionic bombardment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorinated amorphous carbon
Thin films
PIIID corrosion resistance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental apparatus employed for film deposition and treatment.
Film thickness as a function of deposition time. Open symbol represents the thickness of the film deposited by 840s which was not exposed to the SF6 plasma treatment.
Deposition rate as a function of deposition time.
Infrared spectra of films produced with different deposition times. Spectrum of the sample prepared by 840s which was not fluorinated is also presented for comparison.
High resolution C 1s XPS peaks of films prepared with different deposition times. Dots and lines represent, respectively, the experimental data and the spectra deconvolution using Gaussian curves.
High resolution F 1s XPS peaks for the films prepared with different deposition times. Dots and lines represent respectively the experimental data and spectra deconvolution using Gaussian curves.
F/C atomic ratios as a function of deposition time for films produced by PIIID in C2H2/Ar plasmas.
Bode plots for EIS data of aluminum substrate.
Bode plots for EIS data of samples coated with films produced under different deposition times.
Bode |Z| diagram for the system formed by the sample deposited by 840s without exposure to the SF6 plasma.
Total resistance R t as a function of deposition time.

head.ce:title: Effect of the fluorination of DLC film on the corrosion protection of aluminum alloy (AA 5052)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Technological Plasmas, Paulista State University, Experimental Campus of Sorocaba, Sorocaba, SP, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of physics, Federal University of Paraná, Curitiba, PR, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Engineering, University of Campinas – UNICAMP Campinas, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is presented a study conducted on the physical and electrochemical properties of fluorinated a-C:H films deposited onto a commercial aluminum alloy (AA 5052). The coatings were deposited from mixtures of 91% of acetylene and 9% of argon by plasma immersion ion implantation and deposition technique, PIIID. Total gas pressure was 44Pa and deposition time (t dep) was varied from 300 to 1200s. The depositing plasmas were generated by the application of radiofrequency power (13.56MHz, 100W) to the upper electrode and high voltage negative pulses (2400V, 300Hz) to the sample holder. Fluorine was incorporated in a post-deposition plasma treatment (13.56MHz, 70W, 13Pa) generated from sulfur hexafluoride atmosphere. Chemical structure and composition of the films were investigated using infrared reflectance/absorbance spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The corrosion resistance of the layers was determined by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a 3.5% NaCl solution, at room temperature. Films presented good adhesion to the substrates and are classified as hydrogenated amorphous carbon (a-C:H) with oxygen traces. Fluorine was detected in all the samples after the post-deposition treatment being its proportion independent on the deposition time. Film thickness presented different tendencies with t dep revealing the variation of the deposition rate as a function of the deposition time. Such fluorinated a-C:H films improved the corrosion resistance of the aluminum surface. In a general way the corrosion resistance was higher for films prepared with lower deposition times. The variation of sample temperature with t dep was found to be decisive for the concentration of defects in the films and, consequently, for the performance of the samples in electrochemical tests. Results are interpreted in terms of the energy delivered to the growing layer by ionic bombardment.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogenated amorphous carbon films have found many applications due to the association of properties such as high density, hardness, chemical inertness and smoothness [1]. In a general way, they are prepared via plasma techniques including all the CVD and PVD approaches. In this context, Plasma Immersion Ion Implantation and Deposition (PIIID) is an especially interesting tool for the preparation of such films since it enables to control the energy deposited in the growing layer through ionic impacts. In this approach, the samples are immersed directly in a plasma excited from an organic compound pure or mixed to other gases while high voltage negative pulses are applied to the sample holder [2]. In such configuration, reactive species are deposited as in conventional low energy plasma deposition process [3]. However, during the pulse ON time ions are accelerated toward the samples and implanted in the growing film [2]. The energy transferred to the matter via ionic collisions, is decisive for the properties of the samples, including the sp3/sp2 ratio in a-C:H films.

The application of a-C:H films as corrosion protective layers has been studied and some works have shown that fluorine incorporation improves their protective characteristics [1,4,5]. In such cases, fluorine incorporation is conducted using fluorocarbons as film precursor [6,7]. It is well known, however, that fluorine introduction affects, in a detrimental way, the mechanical properties of the films.

In this work, a-C:H films have been deposited from acetylene/argon atmospheres by PIIID. To avoid the degradation of the mechanical resistance, fluorine was incorporated only on the film surface in a post-deposition sulfur hexafluoride plasma treatment. Corrosion resistance of the samples was investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and the results are interpreted as a function of the structure, composition and thickness of the films.

The experimental setup used in the deposition and treatment of the samples is schematically depicted in Fig. 1 . Essentially, it consists of a stainless steel vacuum chamber fitted with two parallel plate electrodes. The system is evacuated by a rotary pump (18m3/h) and gases are introduced via needle valves. The substrates, cleaned in ultrasonic baths, are placed in the lower electrode and the system is evacuated down to 10− 1 Pa, as monitored by a Barocel gauge. Prior to each deposition, the samples were further cleaned in argon sputtering plasmas (70W, 20Pa) for 600s. Subsequently, the deposition plasmas were generated from mixtures of 91% of C2H2 and 9% of Ar (total pressure of 44Pa), through the application of 13.56MHz radiofrequency power to the upper electrode. The sample holder was biased with 2400V negative pulses at a repetition rate of 300Hz. Radiofrequency power was 100W and deposition time (t dep) was changed from 300 to 1200s. During the whole process the chamber walls remained grounded. After the depositions, acetylene and argon flows were interrupted, the chamber was evacuated again and SF6 was introduced up to a pressure of 13Pa. Treatment plasmas were excited by the application of radiofrequency power (13.56MHz, 70W) to the sample holder for 300s. Chamber walls and the uppermost electrode were grounded in this case. In order to investigate the effect of the fluorination process on the properties of the system, one set of samples was prepared in the same conditions described above (t dep =840s) without the post-deposition treatment in SF6 plasmas. Purities of acetylene, argon and sulfur hexafluoride employed in such experiments were 99.8, 99.996 and 99.9%, respectively.

The chemical structure of the films was analyzed by infrared reflectance absorbance spectroscopy (IRRAS) using a Jasco FTIR 410 system and collecting 128 spectra with a resolution of 4cm− 1. Chemical composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Microtech — ESCA 3000 spectrometer. Experiments were conducted under a base pressure of 2×10− 8 Pa using MgKα radiation of about 0.8eV in resolution. No attempt was made to remove surface contaminants. High resolution spectra were calibrated in energy by the reference carbon 1s peak at 284.6eV and fitted with Gaussian peaks after subtraction of the Shirley-type background. Samples prepared onto aluminum alloy plates have been used for both IRRAS and XPS analyses.

Film thickness (h) was determined from a step built onto aluminum during the deposition process. The height of the step was measured in a profilemeter Veeco Dektak 150. At least three measurements were performed in different regions of each sample.

The corrosion resistance of the films was investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). That electrochemical technique can provide information on the presence of defects on the coatings, adhesion and barrier properties, as well as corrosion mechanism and corrosion kinetics of coated metals [8]. EIS measurements were carried out at open circuit potential by applying a sinusoidal signal of 10mV in amplitude in the frequency range from 105 to 10− 1 Hz, using a Lock-in (PAR 5210) connected to a potentiostat (PAR 273A). A three-electrode electrochemical cell was used: the working electrode with an exposed area of 0.78cm2, the saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference (+242mV versus standard hydrogen electrode) and a platinum foil as counter electrode. The experiments were conducted at room temperature in a 3.5% NaCl solution, since sodium chloride is an important component of the seawater and some industrial environments [9]. The Cl− is an aggressive ion that can induce the nucleation of the pitting process [10]. Samples were prepared onto polished aluminum plates for such analysis.

The behavior of film thickness as a function of deposition time is shown in Fig. 2 . The open symbol represents the thickness of the film deposited for 840s without the fluorinating treatment. Comparing the h values of the as-deposited and SF6 plasma treated samples in t dep =840s one can note that the fluorination does not influence significantly the film thickness. In other words, the post-deposition treatment is mild since it does not remove enough material to be detected by perfilometry measurements. Concerning the variation in the deposition time, it is noticed that h increases rapidly with increasing t dep up to 600s and remains practically constant in the range from 600 to 840s. Further increasing in deposition time produces a new rise tendency of the thickness.

As the deposition time is increased, with other parameters kept constant, h is expected to increase, but this was not observed in the interval from 600 to 840s. It should be considered, however, that film growth occurs upon ion bombardment and thus the sample temperature also changes with t dep. In other words, the total energy delivered to the growing layer varies with t dep making the deposition rate (R) dependent on this parameter, as can be seen in Fig. 3 . The deposition rate increases reaching a maximum at 600s and subsequently decreases. For depositions longer than 840s, only slight variation is observed in R. In plasma produced films, the net deposition rate is a balance between neutral/ionized species deposition and ablation [11]. When the deposition temperature is low, the film grows faster due to the low etching rates. In Fig. 3, the rise in the initial portion of the curve is caused by the growth in the availability of precursor species derived from C2H2 decomposition and of reactive sites on the film surface both caused by temperature elevation. The removal of species from the solid phase is not effective in this case. Further increasing in the temperature of deposition, however, accelerates ablation and reduces the growth speed [11]. This explains the fall in R as the deposition time is varied from 600 to 840s. However, the substrate temperature does not grow continuously with t dep since amorphous carbon films usually have high electrical resistivity and therefore develop a positive potential upon ion bombardment [12]. This potential slows down the incoming ions decreasing the total energy delivered to the solid. In this case, the substrate temperature tends to decrease with the augment of the number of ions reaching the surface. The deposition rate enhances again, as observed by the small rise tendency in R in the 840 to 1200s time interval. Thus, according to this discussion, deposition time affects not only film thickness but also its structure: a better organized material is expected to be obtained in conditions which generates low deposition rates but high mobility of the species.

The infrared spectra of the films prepared with different t dep are presented in Fig. 4 . Spectrum of the non-fluorinated film (t dep =840s) is also shown. All the spectra presented bands related to the stretching (2930 and 2870cm− 1) and bending (1440 and 860cm− 1) modes of C–H in CH2 and CH3 groups. Incorporation of oxygen is revealed by absorptions around 1700 and 3400cm− 1, ascribed to C=O and O–H groups, respectively. It was also detected absorptions around 1630 and 1280cm− 1 assigned to C–C bond vibrations.

The detection of oxygen in the films is attributed to the presence of this element in the deposition chamber during the film growth. Furthermore, it is well known that during plasma deposition, free radicals or dangling bonds are left active in the solid structure. Once exposed to the atmosphere, they react with O2 and water vapor incorporating oxygen-containing groups in the film [13]. Both processes can contribute effectively to oxygen contamination observed in this work.

Comparing the spectra in the figure, it is readily observed that a growth in the intensity of the bands is not only related to C–H (2960 and 860cm− 1) vibrations with increasing t dep, which is certainly related to the thickness enhancement but also may be a consequence of the enhancement in the concentration of C–H groups. Changes are also observed in the intensity of O–H (3600cm− 1) and C=O (1700cm− 1) bands as the deposition time varies from 600 to 840s. As film thickness is constant in such range, these modifications are ascribed to alterations in the concentration of the respective absorbing groups in the material.

To understand such results it should be considered that in organic materials, deposition of energy through ionic collisions induces, amongst other effects, bond rupture with emission of chemical species [14]. As hydrogen constitutes lateral or terminal groups in such materials it is very prone to be emitted upon bombardment, generating dangling bonds [15]. As these species are extremely unstable they tend to be consumed through structural organization by crosslinking and carbon bond insaturation [14,15]. However, the recombination process depends on a number of factors including the distance of the radicals, their mobilities and concentrations. It is known that temperature enhancement increases the etching rate and, consequently, radical production as well as their mobility [11]. The recombination rate tends to increase resulting in lower defect concentrations, that is, in a better organized structure [14,15]. But even with increasing the crosslinking and unsaturation degree, active sites still remain in the solid structure capturing atmospheric oxygen as the sample is removed from the vacuum chamber. Therefore, the rise in the intensity of the bands related to O–H groups reflects the enhancement in the concentration of residual radicals trapped in the film structure with the temperature elevation.

In spite of the higher thickness of the film prepared during 1200s, the bands around 1700 and 3400cm− 1 were not detected in the respective spectrum, revealing a depletion of oxygen-containing groups. The low availability of reactive sites to the entrapment of such species after deposition, caused by the reduction in the deposition temperature, is the factor responsible for this result.

It is also interesting to compare the spectra of the as-deposited and the fluorinated films deposited for 840s. Upon fluorination, the large band around 2140cm− 1, related to sp1 C–C bond vibrations [11], disappears while another large contribution around 1240cm− 1 becomes evident. This result is a strong indicative that upon fluorination the sp1 C–C bonds are converted to sp2 and sp3 carbon groups.

Fig. 5 shows the high resolution C 1s XPS peaks for the films deposited with different t dep. All the spectra were adjusted using three Gaussians peaks centered at 284.6, 287.6 and 290.6eV, respectively related to C–H and C–C, C–O and C–F bonds. No significant variation is observed in the spectra with increasing deposition time.

The high resolution F 1s peaks were also acquired for all the films and are presented in Fig. 6 . They were calibrated in energy using the reference C peak at 284.6eV and deconvoluted in one component at 688.1eV attributed to C–F bonds. Inspection in this picture reveals that there are no variations in the shape of the spectra as deposition time is increased, indicating that the surface structure remains practically unchanged in the range of t dep investigated. From such results it is also possible to conclude that fluorine was incorporated in all films.

The F/C atomic ratios obtained from the fitted spectra are depicted in Fig. 7 . As the experimental error of XPS analysis is about 10% one can consider that the F/C ratio is practically constant with deposition time. Furthermore, since fluorine-related bands were not detected in the infrared spectra, one can affirm that fluorine incorporation occurred only in the material surface.

Figs. 8 and 9
show the bode diagrams for the as-received aluminum alloy and for the plasma treated samples, respectively. Analyzing the data in Fig. 9a it can be readily seen that almost all the films resulted in high values of |Z| as compared to that obtained in the aluminum substrate (Fig. 8). Only the sample treated for 1200s presented distinct behavior with |Z| lower than that of the pristine alloy.

Considering the trend of the phase angle with the frequency it can be observed that the curve representing the sample deposited with 1200s is shifted to lower frequencies, indicating a bad performance as a protective coating. On the other hand, films deposited during 300, 480, 600 and 840s presented two time constants: one of them related to reactions in the film/electrolyte interface and the other to processes in the film/substrate interface [16]. The phase angle of those samples, in the high frequency range (104 Hz), is around 70°, that is, the films present a nearly capacitive behavior [5]. On the other hand, the phase angle of the sample treated for 1200s is lower than that found in the reference sample.

Fig. 10 shows the bode |Z| diagram for the sample deposited for 840s and not exposed to the fluorination process. Comparing this result to that obtained in the fluorinated sample (t dep =840s) it is possible to verify that fluorination conferred a higher impedance to the system over the whole frequency range investigated. That is, the post-deposition treatment improves significantly the corrosion resistance of the film.

As proposed by Mansfeld [17], values of the total resistance (R t) can be obtained from bode |Z| diagrams. R t is obtained from the intercept of the horizontal portion of the impedance (|Z|) versus frequency curves in the low frequency range (10− 2 Hz). It corresponds to the sum of all resistances present in the system (electrolyte resistance, pore resistance and polarization resistance).

The values found of R t are plotted in Fig. 11 as a function of t dep. The total resistance decreases with increasing deposition time, except for 840s. The worst result was obtained with the sample deposited during 1200s which is consistent with its low impedance (Fig. 9a). On the other hand, the films deposited for 300, 480, 600, 720 and 840s provided better results. Comparing R t of the as-received substrate with that exposed for 840s to the plasma, an increase of more than 100 times in the corrosion resistance of the alloy is observed.

As the fluorine proportion was similar in all the films (Fig. 7), its incorporation does not explain by itself the observed distinct corrosion behaviors. In fact, other authors have also reported that fluorine proportion does not rule the corrosion response of a-C:H films in corrosive media [18]. Therefore, the interpretation of the results is based on the balance between film thickness, internal stress and microstructure. It is well known that thicker layers render higher corrosion protection [19]. Nevertheless, the structure of the film is also important. According to Sittner et al. [20] and Yatsuzuk et al. [19], hydrogenated amorphous carbons deposited by PIIID possess intrinsic flaws such as micropores, cracks and uncoated areas which allow ions from the solution to reach the substrate surface, initiating corrosive reactions. As the concentration of such defects is low, the permeation of corrosive species to the film/substrate interface decreases. Based on this, it is evident why the films with higher crosslinking degree (600–840s) acted as better protective barriers than the thickest one (1200s). Another important aspect is the rise in the internal stress with thickness [13] that can produce points of poor adhesion where the film can peel off upon electrochemical tests. Despite the higher degree of structural disorder, the good results encountered for the thinner films (300–600s) reflect the importance of the low level of stress for the layer adhesion and consequently its physical resistance.

Powder incorporation during film growth, characteristic of prolonged acetylene depositions, can also account to the fail observed in the longest time deposited sample. It was observed by Mascagni [21] that particulate concentration in films prepared using the same experimental apparatus from C2H2 and Ar (13.56MHz, 200W and 10.8Pa) plasma immersion ion implantation (2.4kV, 30µs and 300Hz pulses) rises with increasing deposition time from 900 to 8100s. Through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) combined with scanning electron microscopy tests Mascagni observed that the worst performance as a protective coating was attained to the sample with the higher concentration of particulates in the structure. Even though the plasma excitation conditions employed in the present work were distinct from those used by Mascagni, the possibility of powder incorporation in the film cannot be disregarded since dust formation was empirically observed in the reactor during depositions. Therefore, a detailed investigation on the influence of particulate incorporation on the electrochemical properties of the films should be conducted.

From this discussion, it can be stated that the layer which presented the best performance attained intermediary thickness and a higher organization degree and that the corrosion protection of films deposited by PIIID is dependent not only on the chemical nature of the monomer but also on the deposition mechanisms.

The properties of fluorinated a-C:H films have been investigated and some remarks can be done. Firstly, a-C:H films presenting good adhesion to aluminum alloys were obtained by PIIID. Fluorine was effectively incorporated on the film surface through a post-deposition treatment. The fluorine proportion is practically the same in all the samples, reflecting the constancy in the treatment conditions. The post-deposition treatment does not affect film thickness but alters the chemical structure and corrosion resistance of the system. Barrier properties depend on association of layer thickness, structure organization and the presence of defects caused by internal stress and powder incorporation. The best performance was attained with the highly organized structure presenting intermediary thickness. In this case, the corrosion resistance increases up to 100 times with respect to the uncoated aluminum alloy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of FAPESP (São Paulo State Research Foundation).
